content.text: CoE Service Catalog for Claude Teams

A Claude Center of Excellence (CoE) Service Catalog is the structured menu of capabilities, resources, and support offerings that the CoE makes available to internal teams across the organization. It transforms the CoE from a reactive help desk into a proactive, scalable delivery engine — teams know exactly what to ask for, how to request it, and what to expect in return.
This document covers what belongs in a Claude CoE service catalog, how to structure and operate it, and how to mature the catalog over time as organizational adoption grows.

Without a service catalog, CoE value is invisible. Teams that don't know what support is available simply don't ask — or they build redundant, inconsistent solutions on their own. A service catalog solves three problems at once:
teams learn what the CoE can do for them — training, API guidance, governance templates, use case reviews, and more.
standardized service definitions reduce inconsistency and re-work across engagements.
SLAs attached to services create mutual expectations and a foundation for measuring CoE performance.
A service catalog is the CoE's promise to the organization. It defines scope, sets expectations, and makes excellence repeatable.
A mature Claude CoE service catalog typically covers four broad categories, each with multiple concrete offerings:
These services help teams build internal Claude literacy and capability:
Domain-specific prompt library access — pre-built, tested prompts for legal, finance, engineering, and HR use cases
Claude onboarding guide — self-service documentation for new internal users
Advanced prompting techniques workshop — few-shot, chain-of-thought, structured output formats
Lunch-and-learn facilitation — the CoE runs monthly sessions on emerging use cases
Enablement services are typically the highest volume in early adoption phases. As teams mature, they request fewer foundational sessions and more specialized or custom enablement.
These services address engineering and integration challenges:
API integration review — architecture guidance for integrating Claude into internal products
Model selection consultation — guidance on choosing between models (claude-haiku-4-5, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4-8) for specific workloads
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) architecture review — design feedback for knowledge-grounded systems
Performance and cost optimization review — prompt caching strategies, token budget analysis, batching guidance
Tool use and function calling design review — structured output and multi-step reasoning patterns

# Example: Technical Support Service Request Form
Service: API Integration Review
Team: Data Analytics Platform
Contact: platform-lead@company.com
Use Case: Customer churn prediction assistant embedded in Salesforce

Context:
- Model: claude-sonnet-4-6
- Expected volume: ~500 API calls/day
- Input: CRM data + free-text notes (avg 2,000 tokens)
- Output: structured JSON risk assessment

Questions:
1. Should we use streaming for this use case?
2. Recommended system prompt structure for consistent JSON output?
3. How should we handle occasional tool_use refusals?
These services help teams use Claude safely, responsibly, and in line with enterprise policy:
Use case risk assessment — structured review against the organization's AI risk framework
Data handling review — guidance on what data is safe to send to Claude APIs given data classification policies
AI policy template library — pre-approved usage policies, system prompt guardrails, and acceptable use language
Audit trail design review — architecture guidance for logging, tracing, and explaining Claude outputs in regulated contexts
Third-party vendor evaluation — structured framework for assessing Claude-powered tools and plugins

Governance services are especially critical in regulated industries. Legal, finance, healthcare, and HR teams typically require risk assessment sign-off before deploying Claude in production workflows.
These services help teams identify and scale new Claude use cases:
Use case discovery workshop — facilitated session to surface high-value opportunities within a team's workflow
Proof-of-concept (POC) facilitation — CoE assists a team in building and evaluating a Claude-powered prototype
Pilot-to-production guidance — support for scaling a successful POC into a maintained, monitored system
Use case repository access — library of documented internal use cases, outcomes, and lessons learned
Cross-team use case sharing — CoE facilitates knowledge exchange between teams with similar problems

attrs.alt: CoE Service Catalog lifecycle and categories

content.text: Each service in the catalog should have a consistent structure so teams can quickly evaluate whether a service fits their need and what to expect. A minimal service entry includes:
Architecture review for teams embedding Claude APIs into products
Any team with an approved use case or active development project
Response within 2 business days; delivery within 10 business days
Keeping this structure consistent across all catalog entries reduces cognitive load and builds trust — teams know what they're getting before they submit a request.
A well-designed intake process is as important as the catalog itself. Poorly-designed intake creates friction that discourages use; too-loose intake creates CoE overload and missed SLAs.
Common approaches, ordered roughly from lowest to highest maturity:
coe-claude@company.com. Works for small CoEs, but loses tracking and analytics quickly.
Google Form or Microsoft Form with structured fields. Better than email; still requires manual triage.
Jira Service Management, ServiceNow, or similar. Enables SLA tracking, assignment, and reporting.
Custom or ITSM-based portal where teams browse the catalog, submit requests, and track status. The target state for mature CoEs.
Most CoEs start with an email alias or form and graduate to a ticketing system once volume justifies the operational overhead of maintaining one.
After intake, the CoE needs a triage process:
Review incoming requests within the SLA window (typically 1–2 business days)
Classify the request against the catalog — does it match a defined service, or is it a net-new request?
Assign to the appropriate CoE specialist based on category (enablement, technical, governance, innovation)
Acknowledge the requester with estimated delivery date
Flag requests that don't fit any catalog service for escalation to the CoE lead — these may reveal gaps in the catalog

# Sample triage checklist (CoE intake runbook)
- name: Review new request
  steps:
    - Check: does request match a defined catalog service?
    - If yes: assign to owning CoE specialist, send acknowledgment
    - If no: log as "out-of-catalog" request, escalate to CoE lead
    - If duplicate: link to existing ticket, notify requester

- name: Acknowledgment template
  send_within: 2 business days
  content: |
    Hi [Team],
    We've received your request for [Service Name].
    Owner: [CoE Specialist]
    Expected delivery: [Date]
    Ticket: [URL]
    Questions? Reply here or ping #coe-claude in Slack.
SLAs make CoE commitments concrete and measurable. Without them, "we'll help you" is a vague promise; with them, it becomes a trackable delivery obligation.
how quickly the CoE acknowledges the request (typically 1–2 business days)
how quickly the CoE delivers the service (varies by service complexity — 3 days for simple guidance, 2 weeks for a full POC facilitation)
Review SLA compliance at least quarterly and adjust targets based on actual throughput. An SLA that's always missed is worse than no SLA — it erodes credibility.
A service catalog is not a one-time artifact — it requires ongoing curation to stay relevant:
Services should be added when recurring out-of-catalog requests reveal a common need
New services require a defined deliverable, an SLA, and an assigned CoE owner before publication
Pilot new services informally with 2–3 teams before formally publishing to the catalog
Document known limitations and eligibility criteria clearly to prevent mismatched expectations
Remove or archive services with no requests in 6+ months
Services that are consistently over-SLA may need to be paused until CoE capacity increases
Sunset deprecated services with a clear communication to teams that previously used them
Redirect teams to replacement services where available
Schedule a CoE-internal quarterly review covering:
Request volume by service — which services are most used?
SLA compliance rate — which services are struggling to meet commitments?
Customer satisfaction scores — where is quality falling short?
Out-of-catalog request log — what gaps does the catalog have?
Capacity forecast — is the CoE team sized to handle projected demand?
The service catalog is a living document. Teams evolve, Claude's capabilities grow, and enterprise priorities shift — the catalog must keep pace.
Key metrics to track and report on the service catalog:
Growing MoM; no single week at 0
First Claude use case live within 60 days of onboarding

Surface these metrics in a monthly CoE operations report distributed to CoE sponsors and steering committee members. Visibility into CoE performance builds executive confidence and justifies headcount investment.
CoE service catalogs evolve through predictable maturity stages. Understanding where your catalog sits helps prioritize investment:
no formal catalog; teams request help via informal channels; CoE responds reactively; no SLAs; no metrics.
core services documented; basic intake form or email alias; informal SLAs; limited tracking. Most early CoEs operate here.
full catalog with SLAs; ticketing system for tracking; quarterly reviews; CSAT surveys; capacity planning.
self-service portal; auto-routing; predictive demand forecasting; continuous improvement loop; catalog integrated with enterprise ITSM.
Most organizations should target Level 3 within 12–18 months of CoE formation. Level 4 is appropriate for large enterprises where Claude CoE demand exceeds 50+ service requests per month.
Practical Example: A Minimal Catalog at Launch

When launching a CoE, resist the urge to build a comprehensive catalog immediately. A small, well-executed catalog outperforms a large, under-resourced one. A practical launch catalog for a small CoE (2–3 FTE) might include:

# Minimal launch catalog (2-3 FTE CoE)
services:
  - name: "Claude Onboarding Guide"
    category: Enablement
    type: self-service
    sla_response: immediate
    sla_delivery: immediate
    description: >
      Written guide covering Claude basics, prompt engineering
      fundamentals, and internal API access setup.

  - name: "Prompt Engineering Workshop"
    category: Enablement
    type: facilitated
    sla_response: 2 business days
    sla_delivery: 3 weeks
    description: >
      Half-day workshop for teams new to Claude. Covers
      system prompts, few-shot examples, and output formatting.
    capacity: 2 workshops/month

  - name: "Use Case Risk Assessment"
    category: Governance
    type: consultation
    sla_response: 2 business days
    sla_delivery: 5 business days
    description: >
      Structured review of a proposed Claude use case against
      the organization's AI risk framework. Required before
      production deployment.

  - name: "API Integration Review"
    category: Technical
    type: consultation
    sla_response: 2 business days
    sla_delivery: 10 business days
    description: >
      Architecture review for teams integrating Claude APIs.
      Covers model selection, prompt structure, error handling,
      and cost optimization.
    capacity: 4 reviews/month
This four-service launch catalog is specific, manageable, and covers the most common team needs without overloading the CoE team. Add services as demand and capacity justify them.

A Claude CoE service catalog is the operational backbone of enterprise Claude enablement. It makes CoE value explicit, creates repeatable delivery patterns, and gives the organization a shared language for requesting AI support.
Structure the catalog around four categories: Enablement, Technical Support, Governance, and Innovation
Give each service a consistent entry: name, description, deliverable, SLA, eligibility, and owner
Build a structured intake process — even a simple form is better than an email alias
Attach SLAs to every service and track compliance rigorously
Maintain the catalog quarterly: add gap-filling services, retire unused ones, and adjust SLAs to match capacity
Start small and grow the catalog deliberately — a well-run four-service catalog beats an inconsistent twenty-service catalog
Measure CoE performance through volume, SLA compliance, CSAT, and time-to-first-value metrics
